retrieved_docs.title: Laboratory Evaluation of Acute Leukemia -- Etiology and Epidemiology -- Epidemiology of Acute Leukemia
retrieved_docs.content: The epidemiology of acute leukemia differs by subtype (ALL or AML) and is influenced significantly by age group. Acute lymphoblastic leukemia: ALL demonstrates distinct patterns in age distribution and incidence, making it a key focus in pediatric oncology while also affecting adults. Age distribution: ALL primarily affects children, with peak incidence occurring between ages 2 and 5. However, it can also present in adults, with a secondary incidence peak observed after age 50. [18] Incidence: ALL is the most common malignancy in children, with an estimated incidence of 3.4 cases per 100,000 children in the United States. [18] Acute myeloid leukemia: AML predominantly affects adults, with its incidence and demographic characteristics closely linked to age. Age distribution: AML is predominantly observed in adults, with its incidence rising steadily with age. The median age at diagnosis is 68.

retrieved_docs.title: Acute Promyelocytic Leukemia -- Differential Diagnosis
retrieved_docs.content: Acute lymphoblastic leukemia (ALL) Acute myeloid leukemia (AML) Aplastic anemia Folic acid deficiency Myelodysplastic syndrome

retrieved_docs.title: Leukemia -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Acute lymphoblastic leukemia (ALL): ALL is seen in patients with the blastic transformation of B and T cells. It is the most common leukemia in the pediatric population, accounting for up to 80% of cases in this group vs. 20% of cases in adults. Treatment among adolescents and young adults is predominantly inspired by pediatric regimens with better survival rates.

retrieved_docs.title: Laboratory Evaluation of Acute Leukemia -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: The epidemiology of acute leukemia shows distinct patterns for ALL and AML, primarily influenced by age distribution. ALL is the most common malignancy in children, whereas AML is the predominant form of acute leukemia in adults. [2] The complexity of the disease is further highlighted by its diverse classifications, including B-cell ALL (B-ALL), T-cell ALL (T-ALL), and various AML subtypes defined by specific genetic mutations. [3]

retrieved_docs.title: Leukemia -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: The production of abnormal leukocytes defines leukemia as either a primary or secondary process. They can be classified as acute or chronic based on the rapidity of proliferation and myeloid or lymphoid based on the cell of origin. Predominant subtypes are acute myeloid leukemia (AML) and chronic myeloid leukemia (CML), involving the myeloid lineage; acute lymphoblastic leukemia (ALL); and chronic lymphocytic leukemia (CLL), involving the lymphoid chain. Other less common variants, such as mature B-cell and T-cell leukemias, and NK cell-related leukemias, to name a few, arise from mature white blood cells. However, with the advent of next-generation sequencing (NGS) and the identification of various biomarkers, the World Health Organization (WHO) classification was updated in 2016, bringing multiple changes to the traditional classification for acute leukemias and myeloid neoplasms. [1] GLOBOCAN, a global observatory for cancer trends, showed a global incidence of 474,519 cases, with 67,784 in North America. The Age-Standardized Rates are around 11 per 100,000, with a mortality rate of approximately 3.2. [2]

retrieved_docs.title: Laboratory Evaluation of Acute Leukemia -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Acute leukemia, which includes acute lymphoblastic leukemia (ALL) and acute myeloid leukemia (AML), results from the malignant transformation of hematopoietic progenitor cells. This process leads to the accumulation of immature blasts in the bone marrow, disrupting normal hematopoiesis. [1] Please see StatPearls' companion resource, " Acute Myeloid Leukemia ," for more information.

retrieved_docs.title: Acute Lymphocytic Leukemia -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: Genomic studies have noted that somatic, polymorphic variants of ARD5B, IKZF1 (the gene encoding Ikaros), and CDKN2A are associated with an increased risk of ALL (odds ratio 1.3 to 1.9). Other rare germline mutations in PAX5, ETV6, and particularly p53 can also strongly predispose to the development of leukemia. Acute lymphoblastic leukemia is not considered a familial disease, and no screening programs have been developed to test for it in childhood.

retrieved_docs.title: Acute Lymphocytic Leukemia -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: Children who are suspected of having acute lymphocytic leukemia should be referred to a pediatric center that specializes in cancer for evaluation and treatment. For children with Acute Lymphocytic Leukemia, induction therapy consists of anthracycline, vincristine, 1-asparaginase, and a corticosteroid. Today consolidation therapy is widely used and includes therapy with a variety of chemotherapeutic drugs with good results.

retrieved_docs.title: Leukemia -- Epidemiology
retrieved_docs.content: GLOBOCAN, which is a global observatory for cancer trends, showed a global incidence of 474,519 cases, with 67,784 in North America. The age-standardized rates are around 11 per 100,000, with a mortality rate of about 3.2. ALL and AML, which are important diseases in both childhood and adulthood, have bimodal age distributions, with CML and CLL mostly in the older age groups. According to the Surveillance, Epidemiology, and End Results (SEER) database, there are 61,090 estimated new cases of leukemia in 2021, accounting for 3.2% of all new cancer cases, making leukemia the 10th most common cancer in the United States. Estimated deaths are about 23,660, which comprises 3.9% of all cancer deaths. Since 2006, the incidence of the disease has increased by an average of 0.6% per year, while the mortality has decreased by an annual average of 1.5%. [6] [7]

retrieved_docs.title: Laboratory Evaluation of Acute Leukemia -- Etiology and Epidemiology -- Epidemiology of Acute Leukemia
retrieved_docs.content: Incidence: AML is the most common type of acute leukemia in adults, with an estimated incidence of 3 to 5 cases per 100,000 adults in the United States. [19]

retrieved_docs.title: Leukemia -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Acute myelogenous leukemia (AML): AML is characterized by greater than 20% myeloid blasts and is the most common acute leukemia in adults. It is the most aggressive cancer with a variable prognosis depending upon the molecular subtypes.

retrieved_docs.title: Acute Lymphocytic Leukemia -- Staging -- T-lymphoblastic leukemia/lymphoma
retrieved_docs.content: Provisional entity: Early T-cell precursor lymphoblastic leukemia Provisional entity: Natural killer (NK) cell lymphoblastic leukemia/lymphoma

retrieved_docs.title: Leukemia Cutis -- Epidemiology
retrieved_docs.content: Leukemia cutis is rare, and the exact data on incidence and demographic predilections do not exist. However, children with congenital leukemia are prone to develop leukemia cutis, ranging from 25% to 30% of cases. [5] Although the highest rate of leukemia cutis occurs in patients with adult T-cell leukemia/lymphoma (ATLL), the incidence of this leukemia is quite rare. Thus the most common subtypes seen in clinical practice are AML and CLL involving 13% and 8% of total cases, respectively.

retrieved_docs.title: Leukemia -- Complications -- Cancer
retrieved_docs.content: Survivors of leukemia are at an increased risk of subsequent cancers. For example, the Childhood Cancer Survivor Study demonstrated that the 30-year cumulative incidence of any cancer after leukemia was 5.6%; the median time to occurrence of the subsequent cancer was nine years. The most common second neoplasms in childhood leukemia survivors are different subtypes of leukemia or lymphoma. [10]

retrieved_docs.title: Acute Lymphocytic Leukemia -- Pearls and Other Issues
retrieved_docs.content: Even after treatment, acute lymphocytic leukemia can relapse. Relapses can occur as far back as 21 years. It is important to address other issues associated with cancer treatment in a young child, including providing psychological support to the child, parents, and family.

retrieved_docs.title: Acute Lymphocytic Leukemia -- Enhancing Healthcare Team Outcomes
retrieved_docs.content: Even after treatment, acute lymphocytic leukemia can relapse. Relapses can occur as far back as 21 years. It is important to address other issues associated with cancer treatment in a young child, including providing psychological support to the child, parents, and family.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 4 -- Level 3 -- Community 2021
retrieved_docs.content: Gilteritinib is a targeted therapeutic agent that may be used to treat acute myeloid leukemia (سرطان الدم النخاعي الحاد), offering a potential option for patients with this aggressive hematologic malignancy.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 14 -- Level 1 -- Community 55
retrieved_docs.content: support to immune‑modulating virotherapy—used for the treatment of hematologic malignancies such as leukemia and other cancers, while also identifying specific contraindications and adjunctive uses.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1291
retrieved_docs.content: Midostaurin is a therapeutic agent that may be used to treat acute myeloid leukemia (سرطان الدم النخاعي الحاد).

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 19 -- Level 4 -- Community 2916
retrieved_docs.content: Dasatinib anhydrous and imatinib are both classified as BCR‑ABL inhibitors. As BCR‑ABL inhibitors, these agents specifically target the BCR‑ABL fusion protein, a constitutively active tyrosine kinase that drives the development of chronic myeloid leukemia and certain acute lymphoblastic leukemias. By inhibiting BCR‑ABL activity, dasatinib anhydrous and imatinib disrupt downstream signaling pathways, reducing malignant cell proliferation and providing therapeutic benefit in BCR‑ABL–positive hematologic malignancies. Their shared classification highlights a common mechanism of action, while differences in potency, spectrum of activity, and side‑effect profiles inform clinicians’ choices of targeted therapy for patients with BCR‑ABL–driven disease.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1287
retrieved_docs.content: Dasatinib and its anhydrous form, dasatinib anhydrous, are both identified as potential therapeutic agents for treating ابيضاض الدم اللمفاوي, indicating that this tyrosine‑kinase inhibitor and its dehydrated variant may be used in the management of this lymphoid leukemia.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 16 -- Level 3 -- Community 2646
retrieved_docs.content: Dasatinib and dasatinib anhydrous are both classified as BCR‑ABL inhibitors, a class of targeted therapies that block the BCR‑ABL tyrosine kinase activity implicated in certain leukemias. By inhibiting this oncogenic kinase, both the standard and anhydrous formulations of dasatinib help to control malignant cell proliferation and are used in the management of chronic myeloid leukemia and Philadelphia chromosome‑positive acute lymphoblastic leukemia. Recognizing dasatinib and its anhydrous form as BCR‑ABL inhibitors underscores their shared mechanism of action and therapeutic role in precision oncology.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1286
retrieved_docs.content: Imatinib is a BCR‑ABL inhibitor, meaning it belongs to the class of drugs that specifically target BCR‑ABL fusion proteins. BCR‑ABL fusion proteins serve as the molecular target for BCR‑ABL inhibitors; they are oncogenic drivers created by the Philadelphia chromosome translocation and are central to the pathogenesis of chronic myeloid leukemia and certain acute lymphoblastic leukemias. By binding to and inhibiting these fusion proteins, imatinib and other BCR‑ABL inhibitors can suppress the abnormal signaling that promotes uncontrolled cell proliferation, thereby providing an effective targeted therapy for patients with these malignancies.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1289
retrieved_docs.content: Ibrutinib is a targeted therapeutic agent that may be used to treat سرطـان الخلايا الليمفاوية المزمن (chronic lymphocytic leukemia), offering a pharmacologic option for managing this hematologic malignancy.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 19 -- Level 4 -- Community 2917
retrieved_docs.content: Dasatinib and imatinib mesylát are therapeutic agents that may be used to treat ابيضاض الدم اللمفاوي, a form of lymphoid leukemia. Both drugs target abnormal tyrosine‑kinase activity characteristic of this hematologic malignancy, providing a targeted approach to disease control and highlighting the role of kinase inhibitors in modern leukemia treatment.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 20 -- Level 3 -- Community 1504
retrieved_docs.content: Dasatinib and imatinib are both tyrosine‑kinase inhibitor medications that may be used to treat ابيضاض الدم اللمفاوي, a form of lymphoid leukemia. Both agents target abnormal signaling pathways in malignant lymphoid cells, offering therapeutic options for patients with this hematologic malignancy. Their use reflects the importance of targeted therapy in managing lymphoid leukemias and underscores the need for individualized treatment planning.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 16 -- Level 3 -- Community 2647
retrieved_docs.content: Imatinib and its mesylate salt form, imatinib mesylát, are pharmacologic agents that may be used to treat أبيضاض الدم اللمفاوي, a type of lymphoid leukemia. Both compounds share the same active moiety and are considered potential therapeutic options for managing this hematologic malignancy.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 3 -- Community 2697
retrieved_docs.content: The classification “cancer; other and unspecified primary” includes malignant blood tumors (الأورام الدموية الخبيثة (SMQ)) as a member of this group, indicating that these hematologic malignancies are categorized under cancers of other or unspecified primary origin.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 2 -- Community 326
retrieved_docs.content: Ponatinib, ponatinib hydrochloride, and asciminib are therapeutic agents that may be used to treat chronic myeloid leukemia (ابيضاض الدم النقوي). Both ponatinib and its hydrochloride salt share the same indication, reflecting that the active compound is effective in this hematologic malignancy. Asciminib, a distinct BCR‑ABL1 inhibitor, also offers a treatment option for patients with chronic myeloid leukemia, particularly those with resistance to earlier tyrosine‑kinase inhibitors. The availability of multiple agents targeting the same disease provides clinicians with alternative strategies to achieve disease control and improve patient outcomes.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 5 -- Level 4 -- Community 2884
retrieved_docs.content: The neonatal disorder (اضطراب لدى حديث الولادة) includes as a specific manifestation the condition of fetal erythroblastosis (كثرة الأرومات الحمر الجنينية), indicating that fetal erythroblastosis is a particular subtype or child entity within the broader category of neonatal disorders. Recognizing this relationship is important for accurate diagnosis and targeted management of affected newborns.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 13 -- Level 3 -- Community 1437
retrieved_docs.content: Imatinib, also known as imatinib mesylate, is a BCR‑ABL inhibitor; it belongs to the class of agents that target the BCR‑ABL tyrosine kinase and is used therapeutically to inhibit this oncogenic driver in relevant hematologic malignancies.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 3 -- Community 1768
retrieved_docs.content: BCR-ABL Inhibitor is a class of targeted therapy that includes ponatinib, ponatinib hydrochloride, and asciminib. Ponatinib and its hydrochloride form, as well as asciminib, are members of the BCR-ABL Inhibitor class and are employed in the treatment of أبيضاض الدم النقوي, a myeloid leukemia. These agents act by inhibiting the BCR‑ABL tyrosine kinase, thereby controlling disease progression. Their shared mechanism and therapeutic indication underscore the importance of BCR‑ABL inhibition in managing this hematologic malignancy.
